name: Blood & Guts
creator: Dorian Yates
summary: Dorian Yates' Blood & Guts HIT as filmed at Temple Gym in the 1996 documentary: one progressive warm-up pyramid then a single all-out working set per exercise to absolute failure and beyond with a partner. Body parts once every six days on a four-day training week (two on, one off, two on, rest). Weekly calendar order varies across interviews; this collection follows the Blood & Guts DVD session order and exercise selections.
principles: One maximum-intensity working set per exercise — quality over quantity.
Warm-ups fire up the muscle but never approach failure (typically 50% then 75% of working weight).
Train each body part once every six days; recovery is when growth happens.
Control the negative, drive the positive with precision (not sloppy momentum), full range of motion.
Every rep has three phases — positive, static hold, and negative — and each can be pushed past normal limits.
Beyond failure with a training partner: forced reps, rest-pause, negatives, partials, and iso-holds.
Log every workout; progressive overload is non-negotiable.

guidelines.trainingDaysPerWeek: 4 training days in a repeating 7-day cycle (2 on, 1 off, 2 on, 1 off)
guidelines.frequencyPerMuscle: Once every 6 days (as stated in the Blood & Guts documentary).
guidelines.warmupProtocol: 1-3 progressive warm-up sets on the first exercise (50% then 75%); 1 warm-up on later exercises when the muscle is already warm — never near failure.
guidelines.workingSetProtocol: Exactly one all-out working set per exercise to muscular failure, then beyond with forced reps, rest-pause, negatives, partials, or iso-holds. Sessions run 45-60 minutes.

name: Heavy Duty
creator: Mike Mentzer
summary: Mike Mentzer's Heavy Duty, as documented in his estate-authorized 'Ideal Routine' presentations (mikementzer.org / Heavy Duty College): a four-workout rotation - Chest & Back, Legs, Delts & Arms, Legs - with exactly one working set per exercise taken to true momentary muscular failure, often via pre-exhaust supersets. Mentzer's own materials disagree on rest between sessions (some articles say 'every third day', others '96 hours (four days)'), so this collection treats frequency as approximate rather than a single fixed number. Late in his life Mentzer taught an even more minimal 'Consolidated Routine' (two alternating full-body workouts - Squat/Leg Press + Pulldown, then Deadlift + Dips - one set each, roughly once every 5-10+ days) as the logical endpoint of the same theory: a distinct, later-stage program, not a variant of the Ideal Routine.
principles: Intensity, not volume or frequency, is the stimulus for growth: recovery, not the workout itself, is when growth happens.
One set to true momentary muscular failure per exercise is not a compromise - justify adding a second set only after the first failed to produce results, never the reverse (deadlifts: near failure).
Only the last, impossible rep recruits the highest-threshold fibers that trigger growth.

As you get stronger the same routine becomes a bigger stress, so required rest increases over time - add rest days as strength climbs, even if that eventually means training only once every 7-10+ days.
Pre-exhaustion (isolation set immediately into a compound set, no rest) ensures the target muscle, not a smaller assisting muscle, reaches failure first.
Judge progress only by whether you were stronger (more weight or more reps) than last time on the same exercise - not by pump or soreness.

Heavy Duty was never one fixed program - Mentzer prescribed different set/frequency combinations depending on the individual trainee's recovery capacity, from 4 workouts/week at 6 sets down to two total sets once every seven days.

guidelines.trainingDaysPerWeek: Reported as roughly every 3rd to every 4th day for the Ideal Routine - Mentzer's own estate-authorized materials disagree ('every third day' vs. '96 hours/four days'), so this is not a single fixed number.
guidelines.frequencyPerMuscle: Ideal Routine: each of the 4 workouts recurs roughly every 3-4 days per workout (sources vary), so the full rotation spans about 12-16 days. Consolidated Routine (later, more minimal program): once every 5-10+ days per workout - estate sources give conflicting numbers ('once/week', '7-10 days', '4-7 days').
guidelines.warmupProtocol: 1-3 progressive warm-up sets per exercise, never approaching failure.
guidelines.workingSetProtocol: Exactly 1 working set per exercise to momentary muscular failure (Ideal Routine), sometimes extended with forced reps, negatives, or a static hold. Pre-exhaust supersets pair isolation into compound. Deadlifts are taken near failure, not always absolute failure. The later 'Consolidated Routine' (Squat/Leg Press + Pulldown; Deadlift + Dips) took this further: 1 set each, roughly once every 5-10+ days - a distinct, more minimal program, not a variant of the Ideal Routine.

splitOverview.focus: Rest roughly 3-4 days (sources vary), then repeat the rotation
Rest roughly 5-10+ days (sources vary), alternating A and B

name: High-Volume Powerbuilding
creator: Ronnie Coleman
summary: Ronnie Coleman — 8× Mr. Olympia — trained six days a week at Metroflex Gym, hitting each major body part twice weekly with heavy compound movements as the foundation. Despite the legendary 800-lb squats and near-800-lb deadlifts seen on his training footage, Coleman himself said those near-maximal lifts were 'mainly for the DVD to make it more exciting' — his actual working sets almost always sat in the 10-12 rep range (occasionally up to 15), for roughly 12-16 working sets per body part per session, not the 16-25-set/4-8-rep numbers a prior version of this entry sourced from an unaffiliated fan site. His own stated principle: lift the heaviest weight possible, with the most correct form, through a full range of motion — chasing a full muscular pump, not a fixed number. The weekly template below (back/biceps and chest/triceps twice; legs and shoulders/traps once) is a common reconstruction; a detailed 2004 FLEX prep log instead pairs back+biceps+shoulders, legs, and chest+triceps each twice — day order is not settled by any single primary source.
principles: Heavy compound barbell movements (squat, deadlift, bench, row) anchor every session — but 'heavy' meant control, not a true 1-rep max; Coleman says he never maxed out.
Work mostly in the 10-12 rep range (sometimes up to 15); the 2-6 rep near-maximal singles seen on his training footage were 'mainly for the DVD to make it more exciting,' not his real working range.
Volume lands around 12-16 working sets per body part per session, not a fixed higher number.
Train each major body part twice per week across a six-day split.
Never train to a true max; prioritize full range of motion and a strict, muscle-focused rep over ego weight.
Chase the pump, not a number: 'I don't tackle a workout obsessed with being able to lift a specific number of pounds for a specific number of repetitions.'

Straight, progressively heavier sets — not documented as a heavy user of drop sets, forced reps, or pre-exhaustion. Rest is reported as roughly 90 seconds to 2+ minutes, but that specific interval comes from contemporary analysis, not a direct Ronnie quote.

guidelines.trainingDaysPerWeek: 6 (one rest day)
guidelines.frequencyPerMuscle: In this reconstruction: back & chest twice weekly; legs & shoulders/traps once. A detailed 2004 FLEX prep log instead shows back+biceps+shoulders, legs, and chest+triceps each twice — day order is not settled.
guidelines.warmupProtocol: Not detailed with specific numbers in any Tier A/B source found; general practice is progressive warm-up sets before heavy working sets.
guidelines.workingSetProtocol: Roughly 12-16 working sets per body part per session, straight progressively heavier sets (his own quoted column: '12-16 sets of 10-12 reps'; Muscle & Fitness stat box: 'Sets per body part: 12-16'). Rest is reported as ~90 seconds to 2+ minutes, but that figure is from contemporary analysis, not a direct Ronnie quote. Warm-ups excluded.

repRanges.range: 5-6 reps near-max — the one lift he regularly took lower

name: Harder Than Last Time (HTLT)
creator: Greg Doucette
summary: Greg Doucette's 'Harder Than Last Time' (HTLT) philosophy, confirmed from his own free YouTube content and official product pages: always aim to beat your previous session — more weight, more reps, better technique, less rest, or more total volume than last time. He explicitly warns beginners against overthinking exact tempo/rest-time numbers, and pairs training with moderate (not maximal) Zone-2 cardio. The full HTLT manual is a paid eBook (Beginner 2-3 days/week, Intermediate 4 days/week, Advanced 5+ days/week, each with 4 program variants); its exercise-level prescriptions (tempo counts, precise rep ranges, warm-up rules) are paywalled — a leaked/pirated copy exists online but was not used as a source, so those specific numbers are not repeated here.
principles: Harder than last time: always beat the previous session in weight, reps, technique, rest time, or total volume.
Training frequency scales with experience: Beginner 2-3 days/week, Intermediate 4 days/week, Advanced 5+ days/week (official program tiers).
Don't obsess over exact tempo/rest-second counts as a beginner — get good at the fundamentals first (his own words, free video).
Cardio is progressed the same way, but at a moderate/Zone-2 pace, not an all-out effort (~116-135 bpm is the example he gives for himself).
Sustainability and consistency beat short bursts of 'killing it' followed by burnout/injury.
Advanced 'all-out' techniques (drop sets, partials, forced reps) are earned, not starting points for beginners.

guidelines.trainingDaysPerWeek: Beginner 2-3, Intermediate 4, Advanced 5+ (official program tiers, gregdoucette.com)
guidelines.frequencyPerMuscle: Not stated in any free/public source reviewed — the paid manual likely specifies this, but it was not used as a source here.
guidelines.warmupProtocol: Not stated with specific numbers in any free/public source reviewed. His own free 'beginner mistakes' video explicitly discourages over-precision here (don't obsess over exact tempo/rest-time numbers).
guidelines.workingSetProtocol: Not stated with specific numbers (tempo counts, precise rep ranges) in any free/public source reviewed. A leaked copy of the paid manual contains detailed tempo/rep prescriptions, but per policy that was not accessed or cited as a source — only the free 'harder than last time' progression logic and moderate/Zone-2 cardio pairing are confirmed here.

name: FST-7
creator: Hany Rambod / Phil Heath
summary: FST-7 (Fascia Stretch Training-Seven) was created by coach Hany Rambod in 2007 and popularized through Phil Heath (and Jay Cutler): after 3–4 straight working sets on most lifts (8–12 reps, 1-3 min rest), finish the body part with one isolation/machine/cable exercise for seven sets ('sevens') at 8–12 reps, 30–45s rest, holding load constant where possible — per Rambod's own protocol text, originally published on his fst-7.com domain and republished by SimplyShredded. Large muscles get FST-7 once a week; arms/calves can take it twice, though Heath himself skipped sevens on arms specifically because they were already his strong point — individualize, not rigid rule. Splits vary — Rambod's own published sample differs from Heath's actual Olympia-era weeks — so the days here follow Rambod's own sample, not a fixed Olympia calendar.
principles: Finish a body part with sevens on an isolation, machine, or cable move — never on heavy free-weight compounds like squat or deadlift (Rambod, own protocol text).
Sevens go last for that body part, after the heavy straight sets — never first.
Sevens: same load when possible, 8–12 reps (M&F narrows to 10-12), 30–45s rest; hold a brief flex/stretch and sip water during rest. Not a drop-set protocol.
Everything before the sevens is normal straight-set training: 3–4 sets, 8–12 reps, 1-3 min rest.

Large muscle groups: once/week with FST-7 — Rambod calls it 'too traumatic' to repeat more often and cites Heath being sore 4 days after an FST-7 back session. Arms/calves: twice/week is fine, but individualize (Heath skipped sevens on arms since they were already his strong point).
If training multiple body parts in a session, expect two or more seven-set finishers that day.
Abs first on days they are trained (per Rambod).

guidelines.frequencyPerMuscle: Large muscles often once per week with FST-7; arms and calves can be twice (Rambod).
guidelines.warmupProtocol: 1-2 progressive warm-ups per exercise; abs first when included.
guidelines.workingSetProtocol: 3–4 working sets on most lifts, then one FST-7 isolation finisher (7×8–12, 30–45s). Do not use sevens on heavy free-weight compounds.

splitOverview.focus: Biceps & Triceps, Calves (Rambod's own published sample — one of 'many other variations,' per his own framing)

name: Golden Era Volume
creator: Arnold Schwarzenegger
summary: Two phases, in Arnold's own account: (1) an early-career full-body foundation — he told Ironman (1994, as-told-to Gene Mozee) that he 'gained most of my weight and massiveness on a program of 10 exercises... three times a week' before switching to a split; (2) the competitive Encyclopedia / Golden Era split from his own book, The New Encyclopedia of Modern Bodybuilding — six days a week, Chest & Back, Shoulders & Arms, Legs (each twice), often with supersets and pyramided rep schemes. The commonly-cited 'Golden Six' (Squat 4×10, Bench 3×10, Chins 3×AMRAP, etc.) has no primary citation found anywhere — every source pointing to it cites The Education of a Bodybuilder by title only, without quoting a page — so it should be treated as an unverified, widely-repeated label, and never as what Arnold used to win Olympias.
principles: Competitive era: each major part twice per week on Chest/Back → Shoulders/Arms → Legs (The New Encyclopedia of Modern Bodybuilding).

In his own words (Ironman, 1994): he built his initial mass on a compact, whole-body program 3×/week, then 'changed over to the more advanced split system' once he'd reached a satisfactory bodyweight — confirming the two-phase structure directly.
Pair antagonists (chest with back) for supersets and density.
Give shoulders and arms their own day so arms are fresh.
Within a session, the book pyramids reps down across sets (e.g. squats 5×20,10,8,6,4) rather than one flat number — competitive volume is high and often supersetted.

'Golden Six' is an early full-body routine widely attributed to Arnold, but no source checked quotes a page from a book confirming its exact exercises/sets — treat it as unverified, not as an early-career fact.
Progressive overload with controlled form; chase the pump.

guidelines.trainingDaysPerWeek: Competitive: 6; Golden Six (early): 3 non-consecutive
guidelines.frequencyPerMuscle: Competitive: 2×/week; Golden Six: 3×/week full body
guidelines.warmupProtocol: Progressive warm-ups on the first compound of each session.
guidelines.workingSetProtocol: Competitive: exercises pyramided across sets (e.g. squats 5×20,10,8,6,4; curls 5×15,10,8,6,4, per the Encyclopedia's own beginner-through-advanced tables) rather than a flat set/rep number — high total volume per part, often supersetted. 'Golden Six' (squat 4×10, bench 3×10, chins 3×AMRAP, BTN press 4×10, curl 3×10, sit-ups 3–4×AMRAP) is a widely-repeated label with no primary citation found in this research pass — present as unverified, not fact.

name: Stimulate, Don't Annihilate
creator: Lee Haney
summary: Lee Haney's eight-Olympia philosophy — stimulate, don't annihilate — confirmed directly in his own words, most recently in a July 2025 YouTube training breakdown (reported by Fitness Volt and republished by Muscle & Strength Clips). Favorite structure: three days on, one off, pairing push with pull movements (e.g. push chest/pull biceps) rather than stacking two press days — a lesson he says he learned after training chest and shoulders together wrecked his own shoulders as an amateur. Calves and abs every training day; pyramid into working sets at ~75-85% max. An earlier interview (Poliquin) phrases the same 3-on/1-off cycle as Chest & Arms / Legs / Back & Shoulders — treat this and the 2025 push/pull phrasing as two eras of the same underlying philosophy, not conflicting programs.
principles: Stimulate the muscle — do not annihilate it every workout (his own repeated motto, on record for decades and restated on video in 2025).
Three on, one off gives each body part three full recovery days while still allowing 75-85% of max weight (his own words, via Poliquin Group).

Pair push with pull (push chest/pull biceps, push quads/pull hams, push shoulders/pull back) rather than stacking two press days — he says training chest and shoulders together injured his own shoulders as an amateur.
Train calves and abs every training day.
Pyramid warm-ups into heavy work; top sets often 6-10 reps.
Uses pre-exhaust (e.g. leg extensions before squats) as part of his own intensity approach, by his own account.
Consistency and longevity beat one reckless all-out week.

guidelines.trainingDaysPerWeek: 3 on, 1 off (repeating cycle)
guidelines.frequencyPerMuscle: Once every four days in the cycle.
guidelines.warmupProtocol: Pyramid warm-ups — light 15, then 10, then 8 before top work.
guidelines.workingSetProtocol: 3–4 exercises per body part; compounds often 4–5 working sets (6–10) after a pyramid warm-up. Calves & abs every session. Stimulate — do not chase failure every set.

name: Growth Program
creator: Frank Zane
summary: Frank Zane's Growth Program (frankzane.com, mirrored on his own blog and Medium account): a three-way split — Pull (back, biceps, forearms, abs), Legs (thighs, calves, abs), Push (chest, shoulders, triceps, abs) — used for the 1979 and 1982 Olympia preps on a 3-on/1-off cycle. This is his post-1978 system; a 2008 Flex interview confirms he ran a different, six-day/week three-way split (Chest&Back / Legs / Delts&Arms, each twice weekly, higher volume) before that. Reflecting on 3-on/1-off afterward, he found it overtrained him within a month and now recommends a 5-5-5-6 recovery cycle instead (3 training days out of 5, three times, then 3 out of 6 once) so you grow between sessions.
principles: Three-way split: pull, legs, push — symmetry over sheer mass.
Train abs every workout (400+ reps, up to ~1,000/day pre-contest, per his own account); include forearms on pull day.
Prefer more rest than a rigid 3-on/1-off forever — Zane's modern 5-5-5-6 cycle, an explicitly retrospective recommendation, not something he used during his competitive career.
Never train upper body two days in a row — an explicit, named safety rule in his own words, not just a general guideline.
Rest ~3 minutes (±30s) between sets while pyramiding weight up in growth-phase training; cut to ~1 minute during pre-contest 'quality training' (his own words).
Stretch the working muscle 15-20 seconds between every set — roughly ten stretches per body part across a session.
Moderate loads, strict form, negatives slower than positives; vary order slightly each session.
Quality and proportion beat chasing the heaviest possible weight.
Log sessions; progressive overload with control.

guidelines.trainingDaysPerWeek: Historically 3 on / 1 off; recommended now: 5-5-5-6 recovery cycle
guidelines.frequencyPerMuscle: Once per pull/legs/push rotation (extra rest on 5-5-5-6).
guidelines.warmupProtocol: One light warm-up set per exercise; never rush into heavy weight.
guidelines.workingSetProtocol: 3-4 sets × 8-12 with moderate loads and strict form. Rest ~3 min (±30s) between sets while pyramiding weight up during growth-phase training; ~1 min during pre-contest 'quality training' (his own stated numbers). Stretch the working muscle 15-20s between every set; negatives always slower than positives; never train upper body two days in a row.

name: One Step Closer Volume
creator: Jay Cutler
summary: Jay Cutler's competitive-era training, documented on his own DVD One Step Closer and in direct interviews: roughly 20 working sets per body part (up to ~30 for back), spread across 3-4 sets each of 5-6 exercises, in an 8-12 rep zone for most of his career (tightening to 10-12 on big body parts and 12-15 on arms/calves after 2004). He explicitly avoided training to failure and skipped drop sets or forced reps — 'I never trained to failure on any set... never felt it was even necessary to try a technique like forced reps' — preferring straight sets and pure volume. The exact weekly split and rest periods are not confirmed by any primary/interview source found, so the days below are a reconstruction of the DVD's body-part groupings, not a verified calendar. In retirement, Jay has publicly revised this down to about 12 working sets per body part (4 exercises × 3 sets) for recovery.
principles: Aim for ~20 working sets per body part in competitive-style training — Jay's own stated career standard (warm-ups/'feel sets' do not count); back can run higher, up to ~30 sets.
Build volume from 3-4 sets across 5-6 exercises, varying angle more than load.
Work mostly in the 8-12 rep range; tighten to 10-12 for big body parts and 12-15 for arms/calves after 2004.
Never train to failure; skip forced reps and drop sets — 'I don't do any kind of advanced-training techniques like dropsets... never felt it was even necessary to try a technique like forced reps.'
Train back twice weekly: one width day (pulldowns/cables), one thickness day (rows/deadlifts).
Post-2003 shift toward free-weight basics (deadlifts, barbell rows, T-bar rows, barbell bench) to match Ronnie Coleman-era mass.
In recovery-focused training, ~12 quality sets per body part (4 exercises × 3 sets) is a legitimate, Jay-endorsed lower-volume alternative.

guidelines.trainingDaysPerWeek: Not confirmed by any Tier A/B source found; the One Step Closer DVD documents body-part groupings (chest, arms, back, shoulders/traps, legs) but not a verified weekly calendar. The days below are a reconstruction, not a documented schedule.
guidelines.frequencyPerMuscle: Most body parts once weekly; back twice (a width day for pulldowns/cables, a thickness day for rows/deadlifts).
guidelines.warmupProtocol: Not detailed with specific numbers in any Tier A/B source found; general practice is light warm-up/feel sets before working sets, excluded from the ~20-set target.
guidelines.workingSetProtocol: ~20 working sets per body part (his own words: 'I'm a 20-set guy'), usually 3-4 sets across 5-6 exercises; back ran up to ~30 sets. Never trained to failure; no forced reps or drop sets — straight sets only. Exact rest periods are not confirmed by a Tier A/B source. In retirement, Jay has revised this to ~12 sets per body part (4 exercises × 3 sets).

name: Lion of Lebanon (1983-era)
creator: Samir Bannout
summary: Samir Bannout — nicknamed the Lion of Lebanon — won the 1983 Mr. Olympia. There is no official branded 'Lion' training system, and no primary source found gives a verified sets/reps table — every numbers table previously in circulation for him (including earlier versions of this entry) traces only to uncredited aggregators. What is verified: a 1981 Gold's Gym training partner (Ironman magazine, eyewitness account) reports Bannout trained a 3-on/1-off cycle he adopted from a late-1970s George Turner program (Chest & Back, Legs, Shoulders & Arms, then rest) — a system he adopted, not invented — and later changed the day order to an AM/PM split. In his own words (Old School Labs podcast, via Fitness Volt), he trains by feel, not fixed sets/reps: full range of motion until the muscle fatigues, whether that's rep 14 or rep 24, with deliberately slow, controlled tempo especially on curls.
principles: Three days on, one day off — adopted from a late-1970s George Turner program (published in a Weider magazine), not a system Bannout invented or branded himself.
In his own words: train by feel, not fixed sets/reps — full range of motion until the muscle fatigues, whether that happens at rep 14 or rep 24.
'Nobody should say 8×10 or 3×12' — reject rigid rep prescriptions (his own quote).
Deliberately slow, controlled tempo, especially on curls (his own on-record description).
Day order isn't fixed — even the training partner who trained beside him in 1981 reports Bannout changed it more than once over his career.
No primary source gives a verified sets/reps table for his training — treat any such table elsewhere as an unverified aggregator reconstruction.
Art and aesthetics matter as much as the weight on the bar (consistent self-statement across multiple video interviews).

guidelines.trainingDaysPerWeek: 3 on, 1 off (repeating) — per a 1981 training partner's eyewitness account; day order changed more than once over his career.
guidelines.frequencyPerMuscle: Once every four days in the cycle.
guidelines.warmupProtocol: Not documented in any primary source reviewed — do not present a specific warm-up protocol as verified for this style.
guidelines.workingSetProtocol: No verified sets/reps table exists in any primary source. Bannout's own description (Old School Labs podcast) is explicitly feel-based, not fixed numbers: full range of motion to fatigue, rejecting fixed prescriptions like '8×10' or '3×12'. Tempo deliberately slow and controlled, especially on curls.

repRanges.range: Not a fixed number — taken to fatigue, which he reports landing anywhere from ~14 to ~24 reps depending on the exercise

splitOverview.focus: Bannout switched to an AM/PM variant (AM chest/back, PM shoulders/arms, then legs) — per the same training partner; day order is not fixed across his career

name: The Blade
creator: Dexter Jackson
summary: Dexter Jackson — nicknamed The Blade for conditioning, not a branded system. Iron Man Magazine (~2004) documents his move from three days/week (~10 working sets per body part across 2–3 exercises) to a five-day split: Mon chest & abs, Tue back & calves, Wed/Thu off, Fri quads, Sat shoulders & arms, Sun hamstrings. On the five-day plan: 3–4 sets on most lifts (5 on squats), mostly 6–10 reps, high-rep extensions/curls for detail, supersets often. Later in his career, training under Charles Glass, Jackson said he shifted substantially toward machines and isolation work and away from heavy free-weight squats to protect his joints and extend his competitive longevity — a documented later-stage change from the 2004 template above, not a separate branded system.
principles: Train smart for longevity — heal injuries fully; ego does not own the session.
Compounds first (squats, bench, rows, chins) — his own words, "the bread and butter... exercises I have to do" (early career).
On the five-day Iron Man split: typically 3–4 working sets per exercise (5 on squats). The “~10 sets per body part” figure was for his earlier three-day routine.
Supersets often; forced reps only occasionally.
Separate quads and hamstrings when legs take too much out of one session.

As you age, be willing to change: later career, working with trainer Charles Glass, he shifted heavily toward machines and isolation work to keep training hard without the joint cost of heavy free weights.

guidelines.trainingDaysPerWeek: 5 (Wed & Thu off in the Iron Man interview split)
guidelines.frequencyPerMuscle: Once per week.
guidelines.warmupProtocol: 2-3 warm-up sets on the first compound of each session.
guidelines.workingSetProtocol: Iron Man five-day era: 3–4 working sets × 6–10 on most lifts (5 on squats). Leg extensions/curls can go very high-rep (up to ~30) for detail. Supersets common. (~10 working sets/body part applied to his prior 3-day schedule, not this five-day template.) Later career, with Charles Glass: substantially more machine/isolation work and less heavy free-weight squatting, at moderate reps (e.g. 4 sets of 8-10 for biceps staples) — not fully quantified into a second program here, flagged as a documented qualitative shift rather than a separate numeric protocol.

name: Old-School Intensity
creator: Rich Gaspari
summary: Rich Gaspari's coaching draws on two distinct chapters of his own career, in his own words: (1) his self-described competition-era mindset of training 'to battle, to war... to total annihilation or total failure' (the origin of this style's name), using advanced techniques to force muscle breakdown; and (2) a separate, earlier lesson from training partner Lee Haney to 'stimulate, not annihilate' — trade brute weight (he once benched 525 lbs) for slower, controlled reps taken to true failure with better form. These are two different, chronologically distinct parts of Gaspari's own story and should not be merged into one teaching. Today's public coaching voice reconciles both: train a muscle hard to complete failure with advanced techniques only about twice a month, then follow with a moderate 'pump' week so the muscle recovers; cap training at 4, max 5, days a week. Pre-exhaustion (isolation before compound) is his most concretely documented current technique, published with a full shoulder-day template on his brand's official blog.
principles: 'Total annihilation' describes Gaspari's own competition-era failure-training mindset (his own Instagram words, via Fitness Volt) — not a branded program, DVD, or split name.

Separately, and earlier in his career, Lee Haney taught him to 'stimulate, not annihilate': less weight, stricter form, full control on every rep (his own first-person blog essays) — keep this distinct from the 'total annihilation' mindset rather than merging the two.
Train a muscle to complete failure (drop sets, forced reps, supersets, pre-exhaustion, negatives) only about twice a month.
The week after a failure/high-intensity week, train the same muscle moderately for blood flow and recovery — growth happens during rest, not the workout.
Cap frequency at 4, maximum 5, training days per week; daily training doesn't allow adequate recovery (his own stated cap).
Pre-exhaust isolation work before compounds, especially lagging body parts like shoulders — no more than once or twice a week per muscle.

guidelines.trainingDaysPerWeek: 4, maximum 5 (his own stated cap, via Fitness Volt IG quote)
guidelines.frequencyPerMuscle: Complete-failure/high-intensity work on a given muscle: about twice a month, alternating with a moderate pump week (his own words).
guidelines.warmupProtocol: Not detailed in any Tier A/B source reviewed for this style.
guidelines.workingSetProtocol: Failure weeks: advanced techniques (pre-exhaustion, drop sets, forced reps, supersets, negatives) taken to complete failure, about twice a month. Recovery weeks: moderate volume for pump/blood flow, not to failure. Pre-exhaust shoulder template (official blog): isolation 4x15-20 and 3x12-15, then compound press 4x8-10 and 3x10-12, then rear-delt finishers 3x15-20.

splitOverview.focus: No Tier A/B source gives a full weekly split for Gaspari — this 7-day layout is a reconstruction, not something he has stated directly.

World-record raw bench press (331 lb / 150 kg) at the 1981 World Powerlifting Championships, before ever competing in bodybuilding.

Self-described past overtraining pattern from her powerlifting years — training daily until sick or injured — now trains "as heavy as my body will comfortably allow" without risking further injury (her own words).
Co-founded and still co-owns Bev Francis Powerhouse Gym (Syosset, NY, opened 1987), an active, ongoing training-world presence rather than a retired legend with only historical material.

summary: Modern mass-monster champion and the first Egyptian bodybuilder to win the Mr. Olympia. principles: "Unlike most bodybuilders, I really prefer machines... you can feel the muscle contraction a lot better on a machine because you can hold the weight and control it down so that you're squeezing the entire time. I don't like using a spotter and I don't have a workout partner, so using machines is a lot better for my workout style" — his own words, Muscle & Fitness/Flex.

Names the Hammer Strength chest press and preacher curl as favorite exercises; his own Instagram-documented arm sessions list barbell curls, skull crushers, EZ-bar curls, tricep pushdowns, lat pulldowns, dumbbell curls, and hammer curls.
Cardio is limited to the StepMill only, prioritized for intensity/effectiveness over duration, per his own words.
Real, filmed, multi-part training documentary exists ("Road to the Arnold," Dragon Pharma, 2020) showing genuine in-person training sessions, even though it is sponsor-branded rather than self-owned.

His books contain many different programs for many different goals, not one universal routine, so a specific weekly split/sets-reps table is not claimed here without a direct page citation.
principles: Authored "Getting Stronger: Weight Training for Men and Women", which has sold over 600,000 copies.

Also authored "Keys to the Inner Universe" (1979, reissued 2015), an encyclopedic reference of 1,200+ illustrated exercises by muscle group and difficulty, plus nutrition, posing, and contest-career chapters — over 250,000 copies sold.
Adopted a lacto-ovo vegetarian diet at age 39, a well-documented personal detail from his own writing.
His books contain many different programs for many different goals (sport-specific, beginner-to-advanced, bulk, cutting) rather than one canonical "Bill Pearl routine".

name: Branch Warren's Training
creator: Branch Warren
summary: Old-school-style heavy training built at his own Metroflex Gym in Texas. By his own account his split changed across three career phases — a 6-day push/pull/legs split early on, a 4-day Yates-inspired HIT split during college, then a 5-day bodypart split once training for IFBB Pro shows. The split and guidelines below follow that final pre-Olympia 5-day template (Muscle & Strength, directly attributed to him), the most complete exercise-by-exercise record found; his own interview quotes are used for the career-wide principles.
principles: "[Bodybuilding is] about getting as much blood into the muscle as possible during training, working the muscle to failure, and recovering through proper nutrition, supplementation, and rest" (his own words).
Trained his whole career at Metroflex Gym in Texas, favoring heavy, controlled compound lifts.
Split changed three times across his career (6-day push/pull/legs → 4-day HIT → 5-day bodypart) as his goals and schedule changed — consistency mattered more than the specific split.
Full control of the weight at all times, even at very high intensity — no sloppy momentum.
High-intensity training partners (Johnnie O. Jackson from 2001 on) pushed his own intensity further.
Even in retirement, still trains hard, but takes 1-2 full weeks off after a big event before easing back in at reduced intensity.

guidelines.trainingDaysPerWeek: 5 in his pre-Olympia split (also documented as 6 early in his career on a push/pull/legs split, and 4 during college on a Yates-inspired HIT split).
guidelines.frequencyPerMuscle: Once per 5-6 day cycle in the pre-Olympia 5-day split.
guidelines.warmupProtocol: Warm-up sets precede working sets on major lifts (e.g. squats, deadlifts); no fixed percentage or rep count documented.
guidelines.workingSetProtocol: Heavy compound lifts anchor each session, followed by several accessory movements per bodypart taken into high-rep pump work; occasional drop set on a session's final exercise.

repRanges.target: High-rep finishers (e.g. leg press, cable rows)

name: Brandon Curry's Training
creator: Brandon Curry
summary: Modern Olympia champion known for being unusually articulate about his own training on camera — he narrates full sessions in his own words on his official YouTube channel, "Brandon Curry All Access." For his 2025 Arnold Classic prep he described a three-day leg emphasis (two quad/hamstring-dominant days plus one hip-dominant day) with upper body trained twice a week, though a complete day-by-day calendar and warm-up protocol were not found in his own words.
principles: Runs an official YouTube channel, "Brandon Curry All Access," narrating his own training in real time (his own words).

For 2025 Arnold Classic prep: "Typically, on a Sunday, I'll have a quad hamstring day dominant. Monday, I'll have a hip-dominant day... Come Thursday, which is usually my high day, I do another quad hamstring-dominant session" — his own words, prioritizing legs because his upper body "responds really well really fast."
Trains upper body twice a week using a horizontal push-and-pull pattern (chest/back pressing and pulling), folding arms and rear delts onto that day rather than giving them a standalone session.
Explicitly credits Lee Haney's "stimulate, don't annihilate" philosophy as a live influence on his own exercise selection (e.g., belt squat lunges).

States a training preference with a reason, not just a number: prefers higher reps on the adductor machine because going heavy on that exercise has diminishing returns for him — "once you go really heavy... there's no point in that."

guidelines.trainingDaysPerWeek: At least 5 named sessions/week during Arnold Classic prep (3 leg-dominant days plus 2 upper-body days); no full weekly calendar confirmed.
guidelines.frequencyPerMuscle: Legs trained 3x/week (2 quad/hamstring-dominant + 1 hip-dominant); upper body 2x/week using a horizontal push/pull structure.
guidelines.warmupProtocol: Not yet verified from primary sources.
guidelines.workingSetProtocol: Deliberately varies leg-press foot position within one session (deep range of motion low on the platform, then shorter range high on the platform); pairs pendulum squats with calf raises as a superset; uses single-leg hamstring curls and higher-rep adductor work by stated preference.

repRanges.range: Higher reps by stated preference, avoiding heavy loading on this exercise.

splitOverview.focus: Hip-dominant leg day (more isolation, less compound work)
Upper body, horizontal push-and-pull pattern with arms/rear delts folded in — exact days not confirmed from primary sources.

name: Casey Viator's Training
creator: Casey Viator
summary: Directly tied to the HIT lineage as the central subject of Arthur Jones's 1973 "Colorado Experiment" — this is fundamentally a documented study of Viator using Jones's Nautilus training philosophy, not a first-person Viator training system in his own voice, and no interview or book describing his own approach was found. The headline "63 lb of muscle" claim from the experiment is disputed even within the HIT community, including by Mike Mentzer, so it is presented here as a contested historical claim rather than settled fact.
principles: Central subject of Arthur Jones's Nautilus-sponsored "Colorado Experiment" (May 1973): 28 days, 12 total workouts, each under 30 minutes, exclusively on Nautilus equipment, personally supervised by Jones every session.
Roughly half the exercises in the experiment used negative-only (eccentric) reps; every set was taken to failure, with minimal-to-no rest between exercises (Arthur Jones's original report).

Was already a Nautilus-trained athlete entering the experiment, not a novice: conventional barbell training through mid-1970, mixed barbell/Nautilus through 1971 (won Mr. America), then Nautilus-exclusive negative-only training through Dec. 1972.

The reported 63-lb "muscular gain" is independently disputed, including by contemporary Mike Mentzer, who stated Viator was "literally force-fed" — the figure likely included substantial fat/water regain after a period of inactivity, not pure lean mass built from scratch.
Later career overlapped with Mike Mentzer's Heavy Duty circle, giving useful pairing context for HIT history alongside the `heavy-duty` entry.

guidelines.trainingDaysPerWeek: 12 total workouts across 28 days during the Colorado Experiment (not a standard 7-day weekly cadence).
guidelines.frequencyPerMuscle: Not yet verified from primary sources outside the Colorado Experiment protocol.
guidelines.warmupProtocol: Not yet verified from primary sources.
guidelines.workingSetProtocol: Every set taken to failure, each full workout under 30 minutes, minimal-to-no rest between exercises, exclusively on Nautilus equipment (Colorado Experiment protocol).

repRanges.range: Taken to failure; roughly half of all exercises performed negative-only (eccentric-only) rather than a standard rep range.

splitOverview.focus: No standard weekly split — the documented protocol was 12 total Nautilus-only workouts over 28 days, not a repeating weekly split.

sources.title: Arthur Jones, "The Colorado Experiment" — original report (via arthurjonesexercise.com)

name: Chris Bumstead's Training
creator: Chris Bumstead
summary: Chris Bumstead's own YouTube channel hosts a full, dated (Nov 2019) first-person walkthrough of his push/pull/legs program, "Push, Pull, Legs Explained" — the richest self-documented training video found for any legend in this research pass, covering all 6 sessions (Push 1/2, Pull 1/2, Legs 1/2) with real sets/reps for most exercises. He has kept publishing training content since, and his day-to-day program is likely updated inside his own paid coaching app (stndrd.app), which this research did not access — this entry follows the 2019 video as its dated, complete primary reference.
principles: "The most optimal way for muscle growth is to train a body part more than once a week" because of the muscle-protein-synthesis recovery window — his own stated reasoning for switching off a once-weekly bro split.

Deliberately lowers total volume per session as frequency rises — e.g. 2 chest exercises for ~6-8 total sets rather than 12-15 — to "give 100% full effort" on fewer sets instead of padding out fatigued, lower-quality volume.

Trains his self-identified weakest body parts (back and biceps, his "pull" day) with elevated intensity and prep — extra caffeine, extra calories — because "I know I need to get in there and fire through that workout."

Uses a self-invented "40-second constant-tension set" on several isolation finishers (EZ-bar curls, pec deck, lying leg curl) instead of counting reps, explicitly to "bring a little bit more intensity."
Runs push/pull/legs twice through per rotation with rest inserted wherever his body (especially his lower back) needs it, not on a fixed weekly calendar.
Warms up legs with a long (~15-20 minute), high-volume, non-specific warm-up plus bodyweight walking lunges before loading weight.

guidelines.trainingDaysPerWeek: 6 sessions per rotation (Push 1, Pull 1, Legs 1, Push 2, Pull 2, Legs 2), with rest days inserted by feel rather than a fixed schedule (his own words, 2019 video).
guidelines.frequencyPerMuscle: Every muscle trained twice per rotation (roughly twice a week) — the core reason he switched off a once-weekly bro split, citing the ~24-48 hour muscle protein synthesis window.
guidelines.warmupProtocol: Leg days: ~15-20 minutes of high-volume, non-specific warm-up plus bodyweight walking lunges. Squat day: 3 warm-up sets (count varies by feel) before working sets. Push/pull days: first exercise done deliberately light as an activation set — no fixed warm-up %/set count given for those.
guidelines.workingSetProtocol: Deliberately lower volume per session offset by training each muscle twice a week rather than once — no single fixed set/rep number applies across all exercises (see repRanges). Several isolation exercises use a self-invented 40-second constant-tension set instead of a rep target; several finishers use drop sets.

repRanges.range: 3 sets of 8-10 reps, then 1-2 progressively heavier top sets
4 sets of 10-12 reps, then burnout reps to failure
2 sets of 10-12 reps, then 2 triple drop-sets

repRanges.target: Timed isolation finishers (EZ-bar curl, pec deck, lying leg curl)
repRanges.range: 2 sets of 40 seconds continuous tension, no rep count

Chris Bumstead — official YouTube channel

name: Cory Everson's Training
creator: Cory Everson
summary: Most dominant women's bodybuilding champion of her era, undefeated across six Ms. Olympia titles (1984-89). In her own directly-quoted words, she trained a rolling push/legs/pull/rest 4-day cycle and tracked "guide lifts" (the heaviest exercise per session) as her core progress benchmark. No numeric claim below traces to her own 1991 book or self-produced training video directly — every set/rep number comes from her own later first-person interview quotes, not a transcribed primary text, so this entry sits closer to a well-quoted reconstruction than a DVD-level primary log.
principles: Rolling push (chest/shoulders/triceps) → legs → pull (back/biceps) → rest, 4-day cycle, repeated continuously (her own words).
Abs trained 3-4x/week as their own category, independent of the push/legs/pull cycle (her own words).
"Guide lifts" (squats, bench press, barbell rows) — a concept from husband/trainer Jeff Everson — tracked explicitly for weight/rep progress as the benchmark for the whole program.
Free-weight-first: deliberately avoided over-reliance on machines, contrary to the era's stereotype about women bodybuilders (her own words).
Deliberate middle-delt emphasis for shoulder width, achieved through exercise selection rather than just adding volume.
Explicit, named overtraining vigilance — she describes concrete signs (insomnia, plateaus, elevated resting heart rate) and a fixed response (time off), not just "listen to your body."

guidelines.trainingDaysPerWeek: Rolling 4-day cycle (push, legs, pull, rest), not a fixed 7-day week.
guidelines.frequencyPerMuscle: Each muscle group once every 4 days via the push/legs/pull/rest cycle; abs trained separately 3-4x/week.
guidelines.warmupProtocol: 3 warm-up sets before the working sets on squats/guide lifts; not documented for every exercise.
guidelines.workingSetProtocol: Descending pyramid from 15 reps down to 8 reps across the working sets, adding weight each set and aiming for a true minimum of 8 reps on the heaviest set.

name: Dennis Wolf's Training
creator: Dennis Wolf
summary: Modern European mass-monster representative of the 2000s-2010s era, with two well-documented, self-described training eras in his own words: a high-volume competitive prime (5-6 days/week) and a current, explicitly recovery-focused retirement routine (3 days/week), which he attributes directly to needing more recovery time as he's aged rather than to laziness.
principles: Runs his own active YouTube channel ("Wolfteam Forum"), describing his current training in real time in his own voice.

Always performs a free-weight compound lift first per bodypart (e.g., bench press for chest, barbell curls for biceps), while strength and energy are highest, then adds isolation/machine work for shape.
Rep ranges scale by bodypart, by his own stated preference: 10-12 reps generally, up to 20 for hamstrings, up to 25 for calves/abs; typically 3-4 exercises and 12-16 total sets per bodypart.
Explicit, quoted pre-contest rule: "In the last four to five weeks before a contest, I increase my overall sets."
Post-competition, deliberately reduced training frequency from 5-6 days/week to 3 days/week for recovery — his own words: "I need more time to recover, and I think I've figured it out, and it works."
Trains calves at least 4x/week as a self-identified lagging bodypart.

guidelines.trainingDaysPerWeek: 3 days/week (Tuesday/Thursday/Saturday) in his current, recovery-focused era, down from 5-6 days/week in his competitive prime — an explicit, stated choice rather than a decline.
guidelines.frequencyPerMuscle: Calves trained at least 4x/week as a self-identified lagging bodypart; other muscle groups follow the 3-day current split.
guidelines.warmupProtocol: Every current-era session opens with abs as a warm-up (his own words).
guidelines.workingSetProtocol: Always leads each bodypart with a free-weight compound lift while energy is highest, then adds isolation/machine work; typically 3-4 exercises and 12-16 total working sets per bodypart, with total sets increased in the final 4-5 weeks before a contest.

sources.title: "Dennis Wolf Feels 30 Years Old at 47, Shares How He's Staying Lean at 237 Pounds" — Fitness Volt

name: Flex Wheeler's Training
creator: Flex Wheeler
summary: Widely considered the most aesthetic physique of the 1990s. Anchored to Flex Wheeler's own words in a live seminar Q&A published as "Flex Wheeler Speaks!" (MuscleMag International, November 1997) — the only source found with an exact sets/reps table, and only for legs and calves. His full weekly split beyond "double split, 4 days on/1 off" is not documented; those gaps are marked honestly rather than invented.
principles: Double split: one bodypart trained in the morning, a different one in the afternoon/evening, usually 4 days on/1 day off, sessions 1.5-1.75 hours.

Legs get 20 reps per set (not 10-12 like other bodyparts) because "they propel you all day" and need more stress; leg day is warmup leg extensions, then squats, then leg press, then a leg-extension finisher — always 20 reps, 4-5 sets each.
Calves 1-2x/week: 3 sets of 20 reps with descending weight (heavy, lighter, lightest).
Back trained "from every angle" with no fixed exercise list — deliberately not a fixed program.
Trains his weakest bodyparts first in the session, when freshest.
No off-season exercise switching — keeps doing what works year-round.
Self-stated philosophy: intensity, consistency, food, and sleep matter far more than the exact number of sets/reps/rest periods.

guidelines.trainingDaysPerWeek: 4 days on, 1 day off — a double split (one bodypart AM, a different one PM/evening).
guidelines.frequencyPerMuscle: Not stated exactly; the 4-on/1-off double split implies roughly once every 4-5 days, but Wheeler does not name a fixed weekly rotation.
guidelines.warmupProtocol: Documented only for legs: leg extensions, 4 sets of 20 reps, immediately before squats.
guidelines.workingSetProtocol: Legs: 4-5 sets of 20 reps per exercise (leg extension warmup, squats, leg press, leg-extension finisher). Other bodyparts: 10-12 reps per set (exact set counts not stated). Calves: 3 sets of 20 reps, descending weight.

repRanges.range: 10-12 reps (set count not yet verified from primary sources)

splitOverview.focus: Legs (warmup leg extension, squats, leg press, leg-extension finisher, all 20 reps) + calves
Not yet verified from primary sources — only "double split, 4 days on/1 off" is documented.

name: Franco Columbu's Training
creator: Franco Columbu
summary: Arnold Schwarzenegger's training partner and a two-time Mr. Olympia in his own right, and one of the most prolifically self-published legends in this roster — at least six training/nutrition books under his own name (1977-1985), led by "Winning Bodybuilding", which has a modern audiobook reissue with Columbu himself updating his advice. Multiple independent secondary sources consistently describe a 14-day training cycle, but none cite a specific page or dated interview, so those exact numbers are presented as book-attributed reconstructions rather than direct quotes.
principles: Self-authored "Winning Bodybuilding" (1977, sold 500,000 copies on first release), reissued by Creators Publishing with an audiobook featuring Columbu personally updating his own training advice.
Authored at least five further training/nutrition books under his own name through the late 1970s and 1980s, more self-authored material than almost any other legend in this roster.

name: Iris Kyle's Training
creator: Iris Kyle
summary: Holds the record for most Olympia titles of any bodybuilder — male or female — in the sport's history. Directly quoted across three separate, years-apart interviews (2008, 2009, 2012) describing a deliberately unstructured, feel-based training philosophy — "K.I.S.S., queen of sticking to the basics" — plus one genuinely published back-day routine with real sets and reps. Her self-reported weekly frequency varies slightly by career stage, which is presented honestly below as genuine change over a long career rather than resolved into one invented number.
principles: "I'm a person who always trains by the basics. It is not a science in my training regimen... I continue to train from heavy lifting to super sets, drop sets, high repetition, etc." — her own words, RXMuscle interview, 2012.
Self-summarized philosophy, her own words: "K.I.S.S. = keep it simple stupid... I'm the queen of sticking to the basics."

Always opens back day with pull-ups as a fixed warm-up, then sequences the remaining exercises by feel rather than a fixed order: "It depends on how I feel and what I did last time" — Flex/Muscle & Fitness, "Ms. Olympia's Back," 2009.

Off-season training frequency is self-reported with some real variance across career stages: "5 days/week, 2 off" (2012 interview) vs. "four times a week, two days on, one day off, then two days on" (2008 interview) — both are her own words from different years, so both are noted rather than averaged.
Contest-prep frequency is consistently self-reported as daily training, including 2 bodyparts/day and daily cardio, across both the 2008 and 2012 interviews.

guidelines.trainingDaysPerWeek: Off-season: self-reported as 5 days/week with 2 off (2012 interview) or 4 days/week in a 2-on/1-off/2-on pattern (2008 interview) — a real, minor variance across career stages, not resolved to one number. Contest prep: daily training, per her own words in both interviews.
guidelines.frequencyPerMuscle: Contest prep: 2 bodyparts trained per session (her own words); off-season frequency per muscle not otherwise specified.
guidelines.warmupProtocol: Back day always opens with pull-ups as a fixed warm-up (her own words); no other bodypart's warm-up was found directly quoted.
guidelines.workingSetProtocol: No particular fixed exercise order beyond the pull-up warm-up — she sequences the rest of a session "by feel," adding an extra exercise only if she feels more work is needed that day.

repRanges.range: 4 sets of 10-12 reps per exercise, except deadlifts at 4x15-10 (descending)

Not yet verified from primary sources — only the back-day routine was published with matching sets/reps detail.

His leg training follows a specific, directly-quoted 3-day split, and his long-running training partnership with Branch Warren remains his preferred style as of 2023. principles: Hosts his own video series, "Hardcore Truth With Johnnie O. Jackson," narrating training lessons from his career in his own words.

A weak bodypart becomes the top training priority — a lesson carried over directly from competitive powerlifting, where weak points show up immediately as reduced total weight ("you'll literally be able to lift less").
Competition-verified powerlifting numbers underpin his approach: 832 lb raw squat, 600 lb bench in a shirt, 825 lb squat in a suit.
Isolation leg work uses a deliberate slow tempo, his own words: "five-count up and five-down, pause up and down, squeeze and concentrate."

Still trains in the Branch Warren heavy-squat style as of 2023 ("Absolutely [I still train like Branch Warren]... yeah, I'm still heavy squatting"), after having also deliberately trained under Chris Aceto, Charles Glass, Milos Sarcev, and Dennis James to compare methods.

principles: "Bodybuilding is a mental pursuit: the body is a manifestation of the brain's work" — his own stated philosophy.

"A secret of mine that you don't see many people do anymore is the Jefferson Squat... Keep your glute muscles tight while you squat down in the bucket... Use smaller plates on the bar, no more than 25s, so you can get down deep enough. The key is to force your knees out" — his own words, posted to his own official Instagram/Facebook.

"I am able to generate great power at 20 reps, and working to failure in this rep range is effective for my body... My legs like this range... I feel more connected than when using lower reps" — his own words, Muscle & Fitness "Training Day" feature.

name: Kevin Levrone's Training

summary: Kevin Levrone has no single official DVD or branded training program, but multiple Muscle & Fitness/Flex features quote him directly on his approach, and he starred in his own 2018 comeback documentary, Born to Overcome. In his own words, he trains compound lifts heavy for 6-8 reps and splits pushing movements onto one day, pulling movements onto another; a dedicated Muscle & Fitness feature publishes his attributed triceps routine. Beyond this, his full weekly split, warm-up protocol, and day count are not confirmed in primary sources.
principles: "Those lower reps always worked for me. I know other people aim for 10, but I grow by going heavy." — his own words, Muscle & Fitness.

"Close-grip bench presses are the single best exercise for packing meat on the triceps." "I always try to go as heavy as possible on free-weight exercises in the 6-8 range." — his own words, Muscle & Fitness triceps feature.
"With pushdowns, I go for higher reps. My focus then isn't on a maximum pump, it's on a maximum burn." — his own words, same feature.

"It always depends on the specific goal. But as a rule of thumb, all of my pushing movements are done on one day, and the pulling movements, on another day." — his own words, SimplyShredded interview.
Starred in his own comeback documentary, Born to Overcome (2018, dir. Vlad Yudin), and ran his own early YouTube channel, "The Levrone Report" (his own words, MEL Magazine as-told-to piece).

name: Larry Scott's Training
creator: Larry Scott
summary: Historically essential as the very first Mr. Olympia champion. Anchored to his own self-authored book "Loaded Guns: Blueprint for Building Massive Arms" (with John Little) for his signature arm-training triset, as corroborated by two independent secondary accounts (Alan Palmieri's Larry Scott profile/interview, and Paul Becker's TrulyHuge piece hosted on Labrada Nutrition's official blog) that both cite the book and agree on the core numbers. No full weekly split was found in Tier A/B material — only the documented arm day.
principles: Signature biceps triset: dumbbell preacher curl and barbell preacher curl alternated set-for-set (elbows in, hands wider than shoulders, full range every rep, no cheating), 6 reps plus 3 "burn" partials per set, ~6 total sets, then EZ-bar reverse preacher curls for burns as well.
Signature triceps pairing: lying EZ-bar triceps extension alternated with kneeling pulley extensions, 8 reps plus burns per set, finished with one-arm dumbbell kickbacks.
Learned the "down the rack" dumbbell drop-set system for shoulders directly from Vince Gironda, crediting it with fixing his own weak-point shoulder development.
Popularized, but did not invent, the Preacher Bench — credits the Easton Brothers' gym as its true originator.
"Success in bodybuilding is 75% to 80% nutrition" — trained under Vince Gironda's/Rheo H. Blair's high-protein nutrition philosophy.

Triceps: alternating lying-extension/pulley-extension pairing, 8 reps + 3 burns per set, ~6 total sets, finished with 4-6 sets of 8-10 dumbbell kickbacks.

A separate, uncited "12-16/9-12/6-9 sets by bodypart size" table circulates on aggregator sites and is not blended with the verified program below.
principles: Founded and fronts Labrada Nutrition since 1995, publishing his own training/nutrition content built around his "Banex" (Balanced Nutrition and Exercise) framework.
12-Week Lean Body Trainer rotates Back & Biceps, then Chest/Delts & Triceps, then Cardio, then Legs & Abs, every 3 training days across a 7-day cycle, for 84 total workouts.
Nearly every strength exercise in the official program is 3 sets x 12 reps — a consistent, moderate-rep, moderate-volume template rather than heavy powerlifting-style loading.
Cardio is scheduled as its own dedicated day within the rotation, not just tacked onto lifting days.

name: Lee Priest's Training
creator: Lee Priest
summary: Lee Priest's early-1990s program is fully documented in a dated, attributed 2003 FLEX magazine interview (his own words, republished by The Barbell) that includes his complete 4-day split with exact sets/reps — "the one young Lee Priest used when he made his greatest gains." His current (50+) approach — full-body sessions 2-3x/week at 70-80% of 1RM — is separately documented via his own on-camera commentary in a 2023 training video published on the Sam's Fitness YouTube channel. The two eras differ substantially in volume and frequency; this entry follows the fully-detailed 2003-documented split as its primary reference and flags the later evolution rather than silently blending the two.
principles: "I believe in the old Arnold style of high-volume basic exercises and heavy weights with six to eight reps. I did at least 20 sets per body part, sometimes 30 or more, and that's not counting warmups." — his own words, 2003 FLEX interview.
"I go by how I feel. I've always been a very instinctive trainer. I never had a set routine in mind before I started a workout." — his own words, same interview.
"Did you push most sets to failure? Yep, every set. If I don't get to failure when I get to eight reps, then I don't count that set. I'll increase the weight and do the set again." — his own words.
"I liked forced reps. I've always had a training partner and that person would help me with the last one or two reps" — the only intensity technique he names.

In his 50s: full-body training 2-3x/week at 70-80% of his one-rep max with lower total volume, and "leave your ego at the door" on smaller isolation muscles like rear delts — his own words, 2023 training video.
Never stuck to one split: "Most often, I'd go four-on and one-off, but sometimes I'd train for two weeks straight if I felt strong."

guidelines.trainingDaysPerWeek: 4-on/1-off most often in his early-career FLEX-documented program (sometimes 2 weeks straight if he felt strong); shifted to full-body training 2-3x/week by his 50s (his own words, 2023 video).
guidelines.frequencyPerMuscle: Roughly once every 5 days on the early-career 4-day split; every session on the current full-body approach.
guidelines.warmupProtocol: Not specified with sets/reps/% in either era's primary source — the 2003 interview only notes his set counts are "not counting warmups."
guidelines.workingSetProtocol: 5 sets per exercise at 6-8 reps, every set taken to true failure ("if I don't get to failure... I'll increase the weight and do the set again"), totaling 20-30+ working sets per body part; forced reps from a training partner on the final 1-2 reps as the only named intensity technique. Current full-body sessions: compound lifts at 70-80% of 1RM for moderate reps, isolation work lighter/higher-rep.

name: Lenda Murray's Training
creator: Lenda Murray
summary: Most-titled women's bodybuilding champion before Iris Kyle (8x Ms. Olympia), and one of the better-sourced names in this batch: she personally authored a 1995 Flex magazine training article in her own written words, has stated a specific injury-avoidance training rule she credits for a career free of major injuries, and remains an active, self-documenting presence on Instagram in her 60s, reportedly posting a current 4-day split.
principles: Personally authored "Lower Body Logic" (Flex, 1995) in her own written words — genuine byline-authored training advice, not a ghostwritten profile.

Deliberately varies squat stance by target, her own written words: narrower stance for glutes/outer quads, wider stance for hips/inner quads, always with full range of motion ("all the way down, touching buttocks to your heels").

Stated personal rule for injury avoidance, self-credited as the reason she avoided major bodybuilding injuries across her career: 12-15 reps/set, no supersets or giant sets, roughly 90 seconds rest between sets, reduced further if struggling to reach 12 reps.
"Listen to your body" is an explicit, stated philosophy tied directly to that injury-avoidance rule.
Still active and self-documenting her training via Instagram in her 60s — a living, currently-verifiable source, unlike most 1990s-era names in this batch.

guidelines.trainingDaysPerWeek: Reported current split: 4 training days across a 2-on/1-off pattern, each session roughly 90 minutes including a 30-minute cardio component.
guidelines.frequencyPerMuscle: Not yet verified from primary sources beyond the reported current split below.
guidelines.warmupProtocol: Not yet verified from primary sources.
guidelines.workingSetProtocol: Personal rule: 12-15 reps per set, no supersets or giant sets, roughly 90 seconds rest between sets, reduced further if struggling to hit 12 reps.

repRanges.range: 12-15 reps, adjusted down if struggling to reach 12 (her stated injury-avoidance rule)

summary: Crossover bodybuilding-to-Hollywood fame who still trains and appears publicly today. His best-documented material is his current (septuagenarian) training approach, published with exact sets/reps by Men's Health at two different ages (69 and 74): a machine- and isolation-focused, low-fatigue maintenance session. This is explicitly his current training philosophy, not a reconstruction of his 1970s Mr. Universe-era program, for which no equally well-sourced primary material was found.
principles: Directly quoted stating a deliberate shift toward isolation work and away from heavy compound lifting as he's aged (Men's Health UK, age 74).
Trains in silence, deliberately, for focus — a specific, repeated, self-described habit tied to a hearing condition, corroborated across multiple 2025 appearances.
States sleep (7-8 hours/night) and diet balance as explicit priorities over training intensity, a consistent theme across multiple independently dated 2025 interviews.

Describes training as lifelong "therapeutic," giving him "a great feeling" and a sense of controllable progress — a framing traced back to building his own weights from junkyard scrap as a poor teenager.

guidelines.trainingDaysPerWeek: Not specified in sources reviewed; likely low-frequency maintenance training given his age and stated philosophy.
guidelines.frequencyPerMuscle: Not yet verified from primary sources.
guidelines.warmupProtocol: 10-minute treadmill walk/jog before working sets (current-era routine).
guidelines.workingSetProtocol: 3 sets per exercise, 8-12 reps, machine/isolation-focused, no heavy compound emphasis stated — this is his current maintenance approach, not his competitive-era training.

name: Markus Ruhl's Training
creator: Markus Ruhl
summary: Extreme-mass case study of the 2000s, with German representation at the top level. Personally wrote (or dictated, "as told to" a Flex editor) an unusually candid first-person training history: he scrapped his first, poorly-designed program after getting no pump in 6 weeks at age 19, then built his own 6-day/one-bodypart-per-day, pump-chasing program for his competitive career, later evolving into a purely instinctive heavy-vs-pump system decided fresh each session.
principles: Deliberately abandoned his first (trainer-assigned, machine-only, no-legs) program after 6 weeks of no pump — his own account of willingness to scrap a plan that isn't working.
Competitive-era program, in his own words: 6 days/week, one bodypart per day, Sunday off, explicitly built around chasing "a great pump with heavy weight."

Later/current approach is fully instinctive, his own words: alternates very heavy, low-rep (1-3) work and lighter, higher-rep pump work based on same-day feel, with no fixed schedule for which is which.
"I like being a freak. It's a compliment when people go 'ooh' and 'aah' seeing my size" — his own words, Flex/Muscle & Fitness.
Trains traps after delts (not with back), preferring machine/dumbbell shrugs over barbell shrugs for a straighter, less-wasted pulling line.

guidelines.trainingDaysPerWeek: 6 days/week in his competitive-era program, one bodypart per day, Sunday off.
guidelines.frequencyPerMuscle: Not yet verified beyond the competitive-era 6-day, one-bodypart-per-day structure.
guidelines.warmupProtocol: Not yet verified from primary sources.
guidelines.workingSetProtocol: No fixed numeric program in his later career — a same-day, instinctive choice between very heavy low-rep work and lighter high-rep pump work. His one published exception is a traps routine with a specific sets/reps table.

repRanges.range: 3 sets of 10-12 reps (dumbbell shrug, machine shrug)

splitOverview.focus: 6 days/week, one bodypart per day, Sunday off (competitive-era program) — exact bodypart-to-day assignments beyond traps were not found in primary sources.

name: Mike Matarazzo's Training
creator: Mike Matarazzo
summary: Cult fan-favorite of the 1990s mass-monster era, "The Boston Mass," who passed away in 2014 — this entry relies entirely on his competitive-era magazine record, which is unusually specific for two body parts: calves and arms. Chest, back, shoulders, and legs training were not documented with comparable specificity in any source reviewed.
principles: "Controlled cheating" on calves: strict form at the start of a set, momentum used only to clear the sticking point at full stretch, never at the end of the rep — his own coined technique and words.
"I train my calves the same as anything else—hard and heavy" — his own words, training calves heavy enough that he "couldn't do more than 10 reps in a set, screaming in agony on the last four."
Trains calves immediately after hamstrings, deliberately, to use existing blood flow/pre-exhaustion.
A boxing-derived, all-day habit of staying on his toes is credited (his own words) as contributing to his calf development, alongside copying gym peers who deliberately walked on tiptoes.

"Larry Scott told me about sideways one-arm dumbbell preacher curls when I first turned pro. I tried them, got tremendous results, and I've been using them ever since" — a direct, quoted lineage claim connecting his arm training to Larry Scott.

guidelines.trainingDaysPerWeek: Not yet verified from primary sources.
guidelines.frequencyPerMuscle: Calves trained immediately after hamstrings in the same session, deliberately, to exploit existing blood flow.
guidelines.warmupProtocol: After each calf set, stretches thoroughly and briefly massages the calves before the next set (his own words).
guidelines.workingSetProtocol: Calves trained heavy enough to fail well under 10 reps; each rep stretched and contracted through a full range of motion with a 1-2 second hold at contraction.

splitOverview.focus: Standing Calf Raise 4x10-13, Seated Calf Raise 4x10-13, trained immediately after hamstrings
Cambered-bar preacher curls, seated alternate DB curls, concentration curls, sideways one-arm DB preacher curls, reverse cambered-bar preacher curls — all 4x10-12
Not yet verified from primary sources with comparable specificity.

name: Nasser El Sonbaty's Training
creator: Nasser El Sonbaty
summary: Huge 1990s frame paired with unusually articulate, multilingual magazine interviews — one of the better-sourced names in this entire roster, with extensive, specific, cross-corroborated direct quotes across multiple separate interviews. His stated philosophy is explicitly anti-routine: exercise order, set counts, and rest periods deliberately change between sessions rather than following a fixed program, guided by two named principles — "consistency" and "intensity."
principles: "The first thing is consistency; the second, intensity" — his own words, Muscle & Fitness, 2002.

Deliberately varies exercise order, set counts, and rest periods between sessions rather than following a fixed routine: "my routine is always changing... the order of the exercises changes, the amount of sets for each exercise changes, and the amount of rest between sets changes."

Explicit two-option lagging-bodypart protocol, his own words: increase frequency to 2-3x/week with fewer but more intense sets per exercise, OR evenly split total weekly volume across three sessions — with a self-diagnosed overtraining signal (chronic fatigue) and a stated remedy (drop back to twice-weekly).

Explicitly prefers free weights over machines because they are the "least favorite" and most painful option, chosen deliberately for that reason: "if you like to have real gains you have to train hard and heavy... go for the LEAST favorite exercises, the free weights... and go for the muscle pain!"

Trains to failure as the core growth mechanism, his own words: "To make a muscle grow, you must force it to go beyond its capabilities. The most potent way to apply that force is to train to failure."

guidelines.trainingDaysPerWeek: 5-6 days/week (once/day) in the off-season, his own words; same weight-training frequency maintained pre-contest, with twice-daily cardio added.
guidelines.frequencyPerMuscle: Quads trained once/week, hamstrings twice/week, with priority-based AM/PM sequencing when both fall on the same day.
guidelines.warmupProtocol: Not yet verified from primary sources.
guidelines.workingSetProtocol: No fixed numeric program by his own stated philosophy — exercise order, set count, and rest periods deliberately vary session to session. Lagging bodyparts get either raised frequency (2-3x/week, fewer but harder sets) or an even 3-way split of total weekly volume (e.g., 10 sets Monday, 10 Wednesday, 10 Saturday).

repRanges.range: Not fixed by his own stated philosophy — trained to failure rather than a preset rep target.

splitOverview.focus: Not directly quoted as a fixed calendar — by his own stated philosophy, session-to-session structure deliberately varies rather than following one repeating split.

sources.title: "Put Size on Your Thighs with Nasser El Sonbaty" — Muscle & Fitness, Kathleen Engel, 2002 (via Physical Culture Study)

name: Nick Walker's Training
creator: Nick Walker
summary: Current-generation mass monster ("The Mutant") who films and narrates most of his own training on his official YouTube channel. This entry follows his most recently reported split — a 5-day "Mutant Split" introduced in November 2024 under coach Kyle Wilkes — reported directly from his own YouTube uploads by BarBend, Fitness Volt, and Muscle & Fitness. He has changed coaches and splits multiple times in recent years, so this is a snapshot, not a fixed lifelong system; the numbers here come from Tier B reporting on his own footage, not an independently re-transcribed video.
principles: Full range of motion and a hard, deliberate muscle contraction take priority over intensity techniques — he deliberately avoids drop sets, rest-pause, forced reps, and partials (reported from his own footage).
Legs and chest trained twice weekly; other muscle groups get 3-4 rest days between sessions.
Trains toward failure, not always to complete failure, to manage fatigue and injury risk on leg day specifically (his own words).
Split and coaching have changed multiple times in recent years (Matt Jansen → Jared Feather/RP app → Kyle Wilkes) — adaptability over dogma.
Mixed rep ranges within a single exercise (e.g. 2 heavy sets at 6-8 reps, then 2 lighter sets at 10-15 reps) rather than one fixed number.

guidelines.trainingDaysPerWeek: 5, with legs and chest each hit twice per week.
guidelines.frequencyPerMuscle: Legs and chest 2x/week; other muscle groups 1x/week with 3-4 rest days.
guidelines.warmupProtocol: Not documented with fixed percentages; leg day includes 2-3 acclimation sets at 10-12 reps around 80-90% toward failure before working sets.
guidelines.workingSetProtocol: Standard straight sets taken toward (not always to complete) failure, full range of motion; deliberately no drop sets, rest-pause, forced reps, or partials.
