name: Paul Dillett's Training
creator: Paul Dillett
summary: Iconic 1990s size and conditioning during a brief but memorable competitive career, "Freak-einstein." Trained on a non-calendar 4-days-on/1-day-off cycle (3-on/1-off during contest prep for faster recovery), often splitting a single day into AM (larger muscle groups) and PM (smaller muscle groups plus cardio) sessions. Committed to substantial daily cardio without, by his own account, losing muscle mass.
principles: "I think the big emphasis is going to be conditioning, conditioning, conditioning... whatever it takes to make sure I have my best condition, that's where I will be" — his own words, getbig.com interview, 1999, stating conditioning as his top competitive priority over maximum size.

"I did a lot of cardio. I was one of the guys who would do two hours of cardio a day... It didn't bother me... I didn't lose any muscle mass from doing cardio" — his own words, a deliberate choice rather than a necessary evil.
"I was always a high-rep guy using moderate weight. It was nothing less than 10 reps" — his own stated rep philosophy, prioritizing strict form over heavier loads at lower reps.

Arm day opens with triceps specifically as a functional warm-up for the rest of the session, then alternates biceps/triceps work: "I loved barbell curls and I loved skull-crushers for triceps... those, along with exercises like one-arm dumbbell curls were the bread and butter for putting size on my arms."

guidelines.trainingDaysPerWeek: Non-calendar 4-days-on/1-day-off cycle, shifting to 3-on/1-off specifically during contest prep for faster recovery.
guidelines.frequencyPerMuscle: Not yet verified beyond the 4-day (or 3-day, in-prep) rotating split below.
guidelines.warmupProtocol: Fasted cardio before the first session of the day (6:30 AM); arm day specifically opens with triceps as a functional warm-up before alternating biceps/triceps work.
guidelines.workingSetProtocol: Multi-bodypart days split into AM (larger muscle groups) and PM (smaller muscle groups) sessions, 3-4 hours apart, each followed by a cardio session. Arm work: 4-5 sets of 10-12 reps per exercise, moderate weight, strict form.

repRanges.range: Never fewer than 10 reps, moderate weight

splitOverview.focus: Rest (3-on/1-off variant used during contest prep)

sources.title: "ABB Discussion: Paul Dillett" — getbig.com interview by Ron Avidan, 1999

name: Rachel McLish's Training
creator: Rachel McLish
summary: Founding icon of women's bodybuilding as the sport's first-ever Ms. Olympia champion (1980, 1982). Her most detailed available numeric routine — the "In and Out" 35-minute whole-body workout, a verbatim excerpt of her own then-unpublished book "TLC: Tighter and Leaner to the Core" quoted directly in a 2006 Iron Man Magazine interview — is from her post-competitive, general-fitness career, not a reconstruction of her early-1980s Olympia training. No primary-source numeric log from her competitive era was independently verified this pass; a secondary report naming a 1983 Muscle & Fitness profile describes higher-frequency, higher-volume competitive-era training, but that report was not independently confirmed against the original magazine text.
principles: Train every bodypart — "you cannot ignore bodyparts," even the uncomfortable ones (her own words).
A short list of effective, concentrated sets per bodypart beats padding out volume (her own words).
Every last set of a bodypart's work ends with a sustained stretch before racking the weight (her own words).
Prioritize midsection/ab work early in a session — it doubles as extra warm-up before power movements (her own words).
Authored her own bestselling books ("Flex Appeal", 1984; "Perfect Parts", 1987) built around correct-form, whole-body training rather than a single fixed regimen.

Reported (via a secondary source naming a 1983 Muscle & Fitness profile, not independently verified): deliberately varied exercise selection and rep ranges across her competitive career to avoid staleness.

guidelines.trainingDaysPerWeek: 3-4x/week with 1-2 rest days between sessions ("In and Out" program, her general-fitness era). Separately, a secondary report describes 4 days/week off-season and 6 pre-contest during her competitive career, not independently verified.
guidelines.frequencyPerMuscle: Full-body every session in the "In and Out" program.
guidelines.warmupProtocol: 3-minute treadmill jog, then midsection/ab work first (doubles as extra warm-up) before power movements.
guidelines.workingSetProtocol: Moderate sets per bodypart per session; every bodypart's final set finishes with a sustained stretch before releasing the weight.

splitOverview.focus: Full-body "In and Out" workout, performed 3-4x/week (midsection, upper body, lower body each session)

name: Reg Park's Training
creator: Reg Park
summary: Arnold Schwarzenegger's own idol and mentor, and the author of one of the most concretely documented Golden Era training systems in this roster: "Strength and Bulk Training for Weight Lifters and Body Builders" (1960), still sold/reprinted today. This pass did not directly quote the original book text, but four independent secondary sources consistently describe the exact same 5x5 mechanic and 3-phase structure, which is treated here as reliable given that unusually strong cross-source agreement.
principles: Self-authored "Strength and Bulk Training for Weight Lifters and Body Builders" (1960), widely credited as the first widely-published, structured 5x5 program — still sold/reprinted today with reader testimonials describing decades-long use.
3 training days/week across all 3 phases of the program — frequency never increases, only the exercise list and rest-period structure does.
Consistently described across sources as strongly opposed to training to failure, for psychological reasons related to near-maximal lifting.
Simple, binary progression rule: complete all 3 working sets of 5, then add roughly 5-10 lb (or metric equivalent) next session — no completion, no added weight.

guidelines.trainingDaysPerWeek: 3 (all 3 phases of the program).
guidelines.frequencyPerMuscle: Each core lift (squat, bench press, deadlift) trained on all 3 weekly sessions in the beginner phase; later phases add more exercises.
guidelines.warmupProtocol: Warm-up is built into the working sets themselves: 2 progressively heavier sets of 5 before the 3 working sets, not a separate warm-up phase.
guidelines.workingSetProtocol: 2 progressively heavier warm-up sets of 5, then 3 straight working sets of 5 at one weight, with 3-5 minutes rest between working sets. Not trained to failure. 3-phase structure (beginner/intermediate/advanced), each roughly 3 months, adding exercises and reducing rest between sets as phases advance.

splitOverview.focus: Squat, bench press, deadlift, hyperextensions — 3x/week, 5x5 protocol
Additional exercises (e.g., front squats, power cleans) added, rest between sets reduced to build endurance — still 3x/week

sources.title: "Strength and Bulk Training for Weight Lifters and Body Builders" — Reg Park, 1960 (still sold/reprinted)

name: Rich Piana's Training
creator: Rich Piana
summary: Hugely influential self-documentarian whose own YouTube back catalog remains a primary record of his training, and founder of 5% Nutrition (2014), whose official brand content describes his approach directly. He deliberately never repeated a fixed weekly split — "variety was his form of progression" — building instead around frequent feeder workouts, an early strength-building phase followed by a pump-chasing phase, and a rejection of "overtraining" as a limit on himself. His most detailed single documented session, the 8-Hour Arm Workout, is one specific, well-documented protocol from his own YouTube channel, not his everyday routine.
principles: Founded 5% Nutrition, built around his own motto: "those who are willing to do whatever it takes to achieve their goals" (his own words).
Deliberately varied his workouts and exercise selection constantly rather than repeating a fixed template — "variety was his form of progression" (his own brand's words).

Feeder workouts — frequent, light, high-rep sessions (2-3 sets of 30-50 reps, or 1-2 sets of 100 reps, superset-style) — to keep blood and nutrients flowing into a muscle between real training sessions.

Build a strength base early with heavy compound straight sets; once that strength ceiling is hit, shift to moderate weight, higher reps, and chasing the pump rather than continuing to chase heavier numbers.
One solid warm-up set is enough — he explicitly rejected doing many warm-up sets that don't contribute to growth.
Rejected "overtraining" as a meaningful limit for himself, while separately acknowledging natural (non-enhanced) lifters need more respect for recovery.
Iso-tension: hard, sustained (~30 second) flexes of a target muscle throughout the day, outside of the gym, as a supplementary intensity tool.

guidelines.trainingDaysPerWeek: Not documented with a specific number in Tier A/B material; described only as high-frequency/high-volume overall.
guidelines.frequencyPerMuscle: No fixed weekly split documented — exercises and target muscles rotated by feel/variety rather than a repeating calendar.
guidelines.warmupProtocol: One solid warm-up set per exercise; explicitly not "countless" warm-up sets.
guidelines.workingSetProtocol: Early strength-building phase uses heavy compound straight sets; once a strength base is built, shifts to moderate weight/higher reps chasing the pump, often in a pyramid (e.g. 20-25 reps down to an 8-12 rep top set).

repRanges.range: 30-50 reps (or up to 100 reps in 1-2 sets)

repRanges.target: 8-Hour Arm Workout (one specific documented protocol, not an everyday routine)
repRanges.range: 16 alternating biceps/triceps supersets, one every 30 minutes for 8 hours, moderate weight

splitOverview.focus: No fixed weekly split documented — he deliberately rotated exercises and target muscles by feel rather than repeating a calendar

name: Robby Robinson's Training
creator: Robby Robinson
summary: Longevity icon, "The Black Prince," who remains an active, self-publishing presence on his own official YouTube channel well into his 70s — a genuine Tier A source still posting as of 2025, unlike most Golden Era names in this roster. His most concretely documented training content is a self-selected "top exercise per bodypart" breakdown, each pick accompanied by a specific, quoted rationale rather than a bare list. No specific sets/reps table was found — his videos document exercise selection and reasoning, not programming.
principles: Exercise selection is justified by secondary benefits, not just target-muscle isolation — e.g., EZ bar press chosen for triceps specifically because it also engages delts/pecs (his own words, from his YouTube channel).
Names chin-ups and T-bar rows as his "top two" back exercises (his own words).
Abdominal training is explicitly V-taper-oriented: "Hanging leg raises... it creates that whole V-taper" — his own words.
Specific form cue for stiff-leg deadlifts (hamstrings): arch the upper back and keep tension isolated to the hamstrings.
Squats treated as a demanding whole-body movement requiring deliberate lower-back fatigue management across a training week: "for a day you wanna [give] the lower back a break" — his own words.

guidelines.warmupProtocol: Not yet verified from primary sources.
guidelines.workingSetProtocol: No sets/reps table found in any source reviewed — his own channel documents favorite-exercise selection and rationale per bodypart, not programming detail.

repRanges.range: Not yet verified from primary sources.

splitOverview.focus: Shoulders: behind-the-neck press, DB/barbell press. Back: chin-ups, T-bar rows. Triceps: EZ bar press. Abs: hanging/lying leg raises. Hamstrings: stiff-leg deadlifts. Calves: donkey calf raise. Legs: squats.

name: Serge Nubret's Training
creator: Serge Nubret
summary: "The Black Panther," whose unique high-volume, cardio-free training philosophy is described in his own words in a direct, English-language interview (Matt Weik/Bodybuilding.com, 2007) — despite a French-language barrier limiting most of his other material. His stated numbers (30-50 sets per bodypart, twice weekly, 12-20 reps, 30 seconds rest) are independently repeated near-verbatim across at least five unrelated secondary sources, unusually strong corroboration for this batch.
principles: "I have been training with 30/40/50 sets per body part (16 sets for arms) twice a week. Sets of 12-20 reps with 30 seconds rest in between sets. So it is impossible to train with very heavy weights. I used to do 225 kg max on bench press and I trained with only 70/100kg" — his own words, direct interview, 2007.
Explicit, self-described trade of absolute load for volume and density — a deliberate choice, not a limitation, confirmed by his own comparison to his much-higher 1-rep bench max.

"I do abs every day for one hour non-stop. I always use high volume and high reps" — his own words; treated daily ab work (reported elsewhere as up to 2,000 continuous sit-ups) as his substitute for traditional cardio.
"A lot of red meat, rice and beans. Sometimes 6 lbs. of red meat a day when training for competition" — his own words on competition-prep diet.

guidelines.trainingDaysPerWeek: 6 days/week, each muscle group trained twice, per his own words ("twice a week").
guidelines.frequencyPerMuscle: Every muscle group trained twice per week across the 6-day split; abs trained every single day regardless of the day's main focus.
guidelines.warmupProtocol: Not yet verified from primary sources.
guidelines.workingSetProtocol: 30-50 sets per bodypart (16 for arms), 12-20 reps per set, only 30 seconds rest between sets — his own words, a deliberate volume-over-load approach.

splitOverview.focus: Abs, roughly 1 hour non-stop (his own words)

name: Sergio Oliva's Training
creator: Sergio Oliva
summary: Arnold Schwarzenegger's chief Golden Era rival, "The Myth," considered one of the greatest physiques of all time. A direct-quote interview excerpt has him describing training to muscular failure — but this directly conflicts with a separate, named-eyewitness account (bodybuilding journalist Bob Zulak, 1984) describing him as "never reaching failure." This is presented here as an explicit, unresolved conflict between sources rather than silently picking one. Beyond that conflict, multiple accounts converge on the same general shape: very high volume, high reps, short rest, and no direct hamstring training.
principles: Describes training to muscular failure in his own words in one interview excerpt: "a routine, for instance, would have you start with a squat to muscular failure... to the point of not being able to possibly do any more reps" — but a separate, credible eyewitness account (Bob Zulak, 1984) describes him as "never reaching failure like many others," a genuine, unresolved conflict between sources.
6 days/week, each muscle group trained twice — consistent across every source reviewed regardless of the failure-training conflict.
High-volume, high-rep, short-rest, "ramping" (ascending weight, same rep target) training style is consistently described, rather than fixed straight sets to one top weight.
No direct hamstring training appears in any documented version of his routine, across multiple independent sources.

Origin story for his signature "Victory Pose," in his own words: developed spontaneously while posing in the 1960s — "I lifted my arms up, and everybody went bananas! From that day on everybody started calling me the Myth."

guidelines.trainingDaysPerWeek: 6 days/week, each muscle group trained twice — consistent across all sources reviewed.
guidelines.frequencyPerMuscle: Every muscle group trained twice per week; no direct hamstring work in any documented version of his routine.
guidelines.warmupProtocol: Not yet verified from primary sources.
guidelines.workingSetProtocol: Given a direct, unresolved conflict between sources on failure training, and a wide reported range of set counts (roughly 20-60+ per session depending on source), no single settled numeric protocol is presented — accounts consistently describe very high volume, short rest, and ascending "ramping" weight schemes rather than fixed straight sets.

repRanges.range: Commonly reported as 15-25+ reps, though exact numbers vary meaningfully between sources.

splitOverview.focus: 6 days/week, each muscle group trained twice — exact day-by-day bodypart assignments were not found with source agreement.

sources.title: "Sergio Oliva Training Routine And Interview" — Muscle & Brawn (direct-quote interview excerpt)

name: Shawn Ray's Training
creator: Shawn Ray
summary: Outspoken 1990s contender who remains an active media personality today, running his own official YouTube channel ("Shawn Ray TV"), primarily an interview-format show rather than a training vlog. His most concretely documented current training philosophy comes from a filmed 2024 joint session with Lee Labrada, covering technique cueing and sequencing choices rather than a fixed program.
principles: "I've always said stretch and squeeze, right? That's my thing" — his own words, describing cueing for cable/crossover-style movements.
Sequences chest day to pre-exhaust with cable work before compound pressing, using bench press as a secondary rather than first exercise.
Explicit self-directed, "anti-trainer" training philosophy in his own words — "own your shit" — preferring to self-direct his own training decisions rather than outsource them to a coach.

No true rest days: even on a maximally fatigued day, substitutes cardio and exercise variation rather than fully resting — "I don't need a day off, I just need to re-change and recalibrate the type of exercises I'm doing."

guidelines.trainingDaysPerWeek: No true rest days by his stated philosophy — a maximally fatigued day becomes a cardio/variation day instead.
guidelines.frequencyPerMuscle: Not yet verified from primary sources.
guidelines.warmupProtocol: Not yet verified from primary sources.
guidelines.workingSetProtocol: Chest day pre-exhausted with cable/crossover work ("stretch and squeeze") before compound pressing movements. No specific sets/reps table was found — his filmed sessions document technique and sequencing choices, not a fixed program.

repRanges.range: Not yet verified from primary sources.

splitOverview.focus: Not yet verified from primary sources — only a chest/upper-body session sequencing preference was documented in detail.

name: Shawn Rhoden's Training
creator: Shawn Rhoden
summary: "Flexatron," the most recent classic-format Olympia winner before the modern mass-monster era, who passed away in 2021. His best-documented training material is his actual, filmed 2018 Mr. Olympia prep under coach Chris "Psycho Fitness" Lewis, captured in a published leg-day workout and a dedicated feature documentary. This material describes what he did and how his coach designed it, not a first-person Rhoden training philosophy, since no direct quote from Rhoden himself about his own training approach was found.
principles: Squat pattern deliberately varied week to week (back squat vs. Smith machine squat) specifically to avoid training staleness, per his coach Chris Lewis's stated rationale during 2018 Olympia prep.
Leg extensions used with drop sets and extended time-under-tension (~21 seconds) as a pre-exhaust/finisher technique.
Trained with a dedicated, consistent training partner ("Stanimal") through his title-winning 2018 prep.
Subject of a dedicated, feature-length documentary ("Flexatron: Becoming Shawn Rhoden," Generation Iron) built around his 2018 Olympia-winning training cycle.

guidelines.trainingDaysPerWeek: Not yet verified from primary sources.
guidelines.frequencyPerMuscle: Not yet verified from primary sources.
guidelines.warmupProtocol: Leg extension drop sets used as a pre-exhaust before the main squat pattern.
guidelines.workingSetProtocol: Leg day (2018 Olympia prep, filmed): leg extension drop sets for time-under-tension pre-exhaust, then a squat pattern varied weekly (back squat one week, Smith machine the next) 4x6-8, machine front squat 4x8-10, leg press 4x10-12, standing hamstring curl 3x8-10.

name: Steve Reeves's Training
creator: Steve Reeves
summary: Foundational aesthetics icon whose career predates the Mr. Olympia contest entirely. Anchored to his own self-authored book "Building the Classic Physique — The Natural Way" (with George Helmer), as reviewed and directly quoted by a third-party source. A separate, earlier self-authored 1951 Your Physique magazine article establishes the same full-body philosophy decades before the book, but that article's exact exercise numbers are contested between two secondary reconstructions and are not used here.
principles: Full-body training, 3x/week (Mon/Wed/Fri), with a day and a half of rest between sessions — never a split routine.
Fixed exercise order every session, in his own stated logic: smaller upper-body muscles first, legs last, so leg training doesn't divert blood away from the upper body for the rest of the workout.
Slow, controlled tempo (roughly 2 seconds up, 3 seconds down) with real concentration between sets, corroborated by an independent 1964 eyewitness account (John Grimek).
Trained the neck as a deliberate, named part of the program — proportion, not maximum size, was the goal.

Wrote about his own full-body training philosophy in the bodybuilding press as early as 1951, decades before publishing his full book in 1995 — a consistent, long-held position, not a late-career reinvention.

guidelines.trainingDaysPerWeek: 3 (Monday/Wednesday/Friday), full body each session, with a day and a half of rest between workouts.
guidelines.frequencyPerMuscle: Every session — each muscle group trained all 3 days/week, in a fixed order (see splitOverview).
guidelines.warmupProtocol: 1 set of 20 dumbbell swings before the main session.
guidelines.workingSetProtocol: Mostly 3 sets of 8-12 reps per exercise, taken with a slow, controlled tempo; neck and calf work use higher reps (~15-20+) and lower set counts.

splitOverview.focus: Full body, fixed order: Deltoids, Pectorals, Lats, Biceps, Triceps, Quadriceps, Hamstrings, Calves, Lower back, Abdominals, Neck

name: Tom Platz's Training
creator: Tom Platz
summary: No official DVD, book, or branded program publishes a verified sets/reps table for Tom Platz's training. What is confirmed in his own words (Instagram videos, a 2024 appearance on Dorian Yates' Shadow Talk podcast, and dated interviews) is a specific training philosophy — reps matter more than absolute weight — anchored on one historical feat: squatting 405 lbs for 50 reps by mentally chunking the set into 10 blocks of 5, with a training partner counting aloud. That feat is a documented anecdote, not a repeatable set/rep prescription, and no verified session template exists beyond it.
principles: "The reps are more important than the heavy weight... a blending of heavy weights on some days with higher reps on some days is really the secret" — his own words.

Verified historical feat: squatted 405 lbs for 50 reps, mentally broken into 10 blocks of 5 reps with his training partner Tony calling out the count every 5 reps — "I can never leave the gym a loser."
"It's a ferocious, overwhelming mentality... I wanted to be uncomfortable" — always tried to beat his own prior best squat session, by his own account.

"I didn't like squatting at all, to tell you the truth... but I love — I'm addicted to the feeling afterward" — trains for the outcome, not because the lift itself was pleasant (2018 Zurich seminar quote).
No official DVD, book, or branded program publishes a verified sets/reps table for a full leg session or weekly split; every specific table circulating online is an uncited aggregator reconstruction.

guidelines.trainingDaysPerWeek: Not verified from primary sources — no dated interview or program specifies a weekly day count.
guidelines.frequencyPerMuscle: Not verified from primary sources. Legs are widely reported elsewhere as trained only once every 1-2 weeks, but no primary interview reviewed confirms an exact frequency.
guidelines.warmupProtocol: Not specified in any primary source reviewed.
guidelines.workingSetProtocol: No fixed set/rep protocol is confirmed. The one fully documented example is a single historical squat set — 405 lbs for 50 reps, mentally broken into 10 blocks of 5 with a training partner counting aloud every 5th rep — described by Platz as a maximal, once-in-a-session feat, not a template for every working set.

repRanges.range: Blends heavier, lower-rep days with higher-rep days — no fixed number given
50 reps at 405 lbs, mentally broken into 10x5

splitOverview.focus: Not verified from primary sources — no official program names a day-by-day split. Only that legs were his signature, most intensely trained body part is broadly attested.

name: Toney Freeman's Training
creator: Toney Freeman
summary: "The X-Man," an aesthetics-plus-longevity representative of the modern mass-monster era and one of the better-quoted names in this batch, across three independent interviews. His defining, most specific first-person material is a genuine injury-recovery story: after tearing his pec minor and being told by a surgeon it would never fully recover, he built a permanent training modification around it.
principles: Explicit priority on symmetry/proportion over maximum size — his own words: "I didn't want [to sacrifice my] package... I didn't want to become big at the expense of [symmetry]."

Permanent post-injury training modification, his own account: after tearing his pec minor (surgeon's prognosis: never fully recover), he adopted weekly deep-tissue massage, heavy stretching, a hard weight cap on incline pressing (225 lb barbell / 120 lb dumbbells), and a shift to unilateral (one-arm) chest work to protect and rebuild the injured side specifically — "focus on feeling and squeezing the muscle."

Named "constant tension" technique applied unilaterally — e.g., four distinct lateral-raise angle variations trained within a single shoulder set (sideways, slightly ahead of shoulder line, slightly behind), each for 10-12 reps.
Follows a 13-14-day training cycle before taking 1-2 rest days, with each bodypart trained roughly twice within that cycle, twice daily; cardio capped at 3x/week for 30 minutes.

guidelines.trainingDaysPerWeek: 13-14-day training cycle before 1-2 rest days, repeating; each bodypart trained roughly twice within that cycle, twice daily.
guidelines.frequencyPerMuscle: Each bodypart trained twice per week, twice a day.
guidelines.warmupProtocol: Not yet verified from primary sources.
guidelines.workingSetProtocol: Post-pec-injury: hard weight cap on incline pressing (225 lb barbell / 120 lb dumbbells), shift to unilateral chest work, weekly deep-tissue massage and stretching as ongoing maintenance, not just short-term rehab. Shoulders use unilateral "constant tension" with multiple angle variations per set.

repRanges.range: 10-12 reps per angle variation, within the same overall set

splitOverview.focus: 13-14-day cycle, each bodypart trained roughly twice, twice daily, plus cardio 3x/week (30 min) — exact day-by-day bodypart assignments were not found in primary sources.

sources.title: "Toney Freeman Full Interview | Tall Bodybuilders, Health Scares, & Victor Martinez" — Generation Iron

name: Victor Martinez's Training
creator: Victor Martinez
summary: "The Dominican Dominator," one of the best-quoted names in this research batch, with consistent, cross-corroborated principles across at least four separate interviews spanning 2007-2020s. His defining, most-repeated principle is prioritization: whatever bodypart is the day's main focus is trained first, while freshest, for maximum pre-exhaustion and targeted development. A detailed, dated current routine documents specific weights/sets/reps across a 4-day rotating split — one of the more complete programs found in this entire batch.
principles: Explicit "prioritize" rule: the day's target bodypart is always trained first, while freshest — hamstrings before quads, incline before flat bench, side laterals before other shoulder work — explicitly for pre-exhaustion and targeted focus.

Learned proper full-range-of-motion form by directly studying Arnold Schwarzenegger's technique in Pumping Iron and copying experienced lifters in his own gym: "I learned to train the right way, lifting through a full range of motion and never using a weight that I couldn't handle."

Flexible 5-day/week frequency, his own words: "I train five days a week... The cycle can vary from three days on/two days off to four days on/one day off to five days straight. It all depends on how I'm feeling that week."
Calves trained every 2 days; abs explicitly capped at 2x/week: "If I train my abs more than that, I find that they tend to grow too big."
Does all the basic compound lifts (bench, squat, deadlift) but sequences them deliberately within a session rather than always leading with them — e.g., squats last on leg day.
Trains partly at Bev Francis's Powerhouse Gym in Syosset, NY, directly connecting this entry to `bev-francis`.

guidelines.trainingDaysPerWeek: 5 days/week, with the exact on/off pattern (3-on/2-off, 4-on/1-off, or 5 straight days) chosen week to week based on how he's feeling — his current documented routine uses a 4-days-on/1-off rotating split.
guidelines.frequencyPerMuscle: Calves trained every 2 days; abs capped at 2x/week to avoid over-developing them.
guidelines.warmupProtocol: Not yet verified from primary sources beyond the general prioritization logic (training the target bodypart first while freshest).
guidelines.workingSetProtocol: Whatever bodypart is the session's main focus is trained first. Current documented routine (Muscle Insider): Day 1 shoulders/triceps/abs (barbell shoulder press to 225x10, shrugs with 315, side laterals to 50-lb DBs); Day 2 quads/hamstrings/calves (leg extension pre-exhaust, barbell squat to 405x8-10 then drop set to 225 to failure, leg press to 11 plates/side x8-10); Day 3 chest/biceps/forearms/abs (incline DB flyes to 70s x10, incline barbell press to 225x10, flat DB press 4 sets w/ drop set, 3 supersets of decline DB press/DB pullover).

repRanges.range: 405 lb x 8-10, then drop set to 225 lb to failure

splitOverview.focus: Not itemized with the same specificity as days 1-3 in sources reviewed (likely back, to complete the 4-day rotation)

sources.title: "The Dominican Dominator's Massive Plan" — Muscle & Fitness/Flex (direct quotes)
"Victor Martinez – Relentless" — Muscle Insider (dated current routine with weights/sets/reps)

name: Vince Gironda's Training
creator: Vince Gironda
summary: Hugely influential pre-Olympia training theorist who ran his own Hollywood gym and mail-order courses. Anchored to his own 1984 book "Unleashing the Wild Physique" (with Robert Kennedy) for the 8x8 system, and to two Iron Man Magazine articles he wrote himself in 1983 for his deliberate "muscle confusion" philosophy. The one fully-specified, exercise-by-exercise routine — his 12-move beginner full-body program — comes from an Iron Man feature drawing on student Alan Palmieri's documented compilation of Vince's methods (Tier B, not his own literal words, but historically specific).
principles: Deliberately varied training systems over time ("muscle confusion") rather than running one fixed program — rotated between named schemes like 3x8, 5x5, 6x6, and 10x10.

The 8x8 system (8 sets x 8 reps, light-enough weight, minimal rest) was his signature "honest" program, reached only after a beginner ramp: month 1 at 3x8, month 2 at 5x5, month 3 at 6x6 (held 3+ months) before attempting 8x8.

Beginners train the same 12-exercise full-body routine daily (6 days/week, resting only Sunday) for the first month, ramping from 1 to 3 sets/exercise, then move to 3x/week and hold that routine roughly 6 months.
Banned the plain barbell bench press and barbell back squat at his own gym; used the neck/guillotine press and wide dips for chest, prioritizing feeling the target muscle over moving maximum weight.
No music in the gym — training required total mental focus and visualization.

guidelines.trainingDaysPerWeek: Beginners: 6 days/week (rest Sunday) for the first month, then 3x/week (held ~6 months). Advanced 8x8: a repeating body-part-split cycle; exact weekly day count not specified beyond a 3-day-per-cycle example (his pupil Mohamed Makkawy's pre-contest routine).
guidelines.frequencyPerMuscle: Beginners: every training day for the first month, then every session on a 3x/week full-body plan. Advanced 8x8 (Makkawy example): each of 3 bodyparts trained once every 3-day cycle.
guidelines.warmupProtocol: Not yet verified from primary sources.
guidelines.workingSetProtocol: Beginner ramp: week 1 = 1 set/exercise, week 2 = 2 sets, week 3 = 3 sets, week 4+ = 3 sets/exercise held ~6 months. Advanced 8x8: 8 sets of 8 reps per exercise, 60-70% of 1RM, minimal rest, 2-4 exercises per muscle group.

repRanges.range: 10-20 reps depending on exercise, 1-3 sets ramping over 4 weeks

splitOverview.focus: 12-exercise full-body list: short-pulley rows, lateral raises, triceps pushdowns, barbell curls, leg extensions, leg curls, calf raises, crunches, plus 4 exercises not yet in the exercise library
Only bodypart groupings for days 2-3 are documented (arms & calves; upper legs & abs, per pupil Mohamed Makkawy's pre-contest example) — day 1 is not specified in any source reviewed.

name: Vince Taylor's Training
creator: Vince Taylor
summary: Longevity and symmetry-era representative who won the Masters Olympia — one of the best-sourced names in this batch, with a named, self-described "push-pull" training system consistently corroborated across at least three direct-quote interviews spanning 2002-2014, in continuous use since 1984. A tricep tendon injury in the 1990s led him to invent his own "Precision Grips" training accessories from a rehab discovery.
principles: Named, self-described "push-pull" system in continuous use since 1984, his own words: "I went back to the patented Vince Taylor push-pull workouts... That's what I'd been doing since 1984."
Push days (chest/shoulders/triceps) Mon/Thu, Pull days (back/biceps/traps) Tue/Fri, a second Push block (legs/abs) Wed/Sat, often training all 7 days.

2002 Masters Olympia comeback rule, his own words: 3 exercises per bodypart, 5 sets per exercise, 10-12 reps on a good day, 6-8 on a bad day — explicit emphasis on "contracting the muscle and controlling the weight" over chasing load, with the quoted rule "Don't try to be bigger than you ever were."
Biceps training explicitly capped at moderate-to-medium-heavy weight, max 5 sets of 10-12 reps: "anything above [that] would be overtraining."

Invented his own "Precision Grips" training accessory after a tricep tendon tear in the 1990s, discovered during rehab tricep pushdowns: "My concept is to go to the gym to create muscle stimulation and activate muscle. I want to PULL the resistance into my muscles."
Developed a second, later-career named system ("J.R.P.," Joint Rotation Principle) as of 2014, an addition to his push-pull framework rather than a replacement for it.

guidelines.trainingDaysPerWeek: 6 days/week during focused prep (often training all 7), two sessions per day (one bodypart AM, one PM) during comeback/contest prep specifically.
guidelines.frequencyPerMuscle: Push (chest/shoulders/triceps) and Pull (back/biceps/traps) days, plus a second Push block for legs/abs, within a 6-7 day rotation.
guidelines.warmupProtocol: Not yet verified from primary sources.
guidelines.workingSetProtocol: Comeback-specific rule: 3 exercises per bodypart, 5 sets per exercise, 10-12 reps on a good day / 6-8 on a bad day, prioritizing muscle contraction and control over chasing his old competitive size. Biceps explicitly capped at moderate-to-medium-heavy weight, max 5x10-12.

repRanges.range: 10-12 reps (good day) / 6-8 reps (bad day), 5 sets per exercise
Max 5 sets of 10-12 reps, moderate-to-medium-heavy weight only

sources.title: "The Road Back" — Muscle & Fitness, 2002 comeback feature (direct quotes)
